feat: cross-pane correlation engine with chi-squared co-occurrence (wa-3pr0)

Statistical engine detecting correlated event patterns across panes using
chi-squared tests on co-occurrence matrices. 23 tests.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-1qz1.10","title":"Agent session behavioral DNA — fingerprinting, clustering, predictions","description":"## Goal
Compute compact behavioral fingerprints ("Session DNA") for each agent session, enabling similarity clustering, anomaly detection, and predictions like "sessions similar to this one usually take 4 hours."

## Background & Motivation
agent_sessions table in storage.rs already tracks: agent_type, tokens, cost, model_name. But it doesn't capture BEHAVIORAL patterns: how the agent worked, what tools it used, when it was active vs idle, what errors it encountered.

Session DNA is a compact feature vector that captures the behavioral signature of a session, enabling:
1. Clustering: "This session behaves like sessions working on auth features"
2. Anomaly detection: "This session's pattern is 3 sigma from any cluster -- something unusual"
3. Duration prediction: "Similar sessions took 2-6 hours"
4. Post-mortem: "Why did this session fail? Compare DNA to successful sessions"

## Technical Design

### Feature Vector (DNA)
```rust
struct SessionDNA {
    // Activity profile (normalized to session duration)
    active_fraction: f32,      // Fraction of time with output
    idle_fraction: f32,        // Fraction with no output
    burst_count: u32,          // Number of activity bursts
    mean_burst_duration_s: f32,
    mean_idle_duration_s: f32,

    // Output characteristics
    total_lines: u64,
    output_entropy: f32,       // Shannon entropy of output
    unique_line_ratio: f32,    // Fraction of unique lines
    ansi_density: f32,         // Fraction of ANSI escapes
    mean_line_length: f32,

    // Tool usage (from pattern detections)
    tool_call_count: u32,      // e.g., claude_code.tool_use detections
    error_count: u32,          // Error pattern detections
    rate_limit_count: u32,     // Rate limit detections
    compaction_count: u32,     // Context compaction events

    // Timing
    duration_hours: f32,
    time_to_first_error: Option<f32>,  // Hours until first error
    tokens_per_hour: f32,

    // Embedding (8-dimensional PCA of above features)
    embedding: [f32; 8],
}
```

### DNA Computation
Computed incrementally during session lifetime:
- Activity metrics: from capture pipeline (tailer.rs)
- Output characteristics: from BOCPD feature computation
- Tool usage: from pattern detection events
- Embedding: PCA fitted on historical sessions, applied online

### Pre-PCA Fallback (Cold Start)
Before min_sessions_for_pca (50) sessions are collected:
- Use raw normalized feature vector directly (no PCA projection)
- Similarity computed on raw features with L2 distance (Euclidean)
- Features are z-score normalized using running mean/variance
- Once 50+ sessions exist, fit PCA on accumulated data, project all existing sessions
- From that point forward, new sessions use the fitted PCA
- PCA is periodically re-fitted (every 100 new sessions) to incorporate distribution shift

This ensures Session DNA is useful from day one, with increasing accuracy as data accumulates.

### Similarity
Cosine similarity on the embedding vector:
  sim(a, b) = a dot b / (|a| * |b|)

Sessions with sim > 0.85 are in the same behavioral cluster.

### Predictions
For a running session, find the K most similar completed sessions:
- Duration prediction: median duration of K neighbors +/- IQR
- Success probability: fraction of K neighbors that completed successfully
- Common failure modes: most frequent error types in K neighbors that failed

### Implementation Location
- New file: crates/wa-core/src/session_dna.rs
- Integration: ingest.rs updates DNA incrementally per capture
- Storage: session_dna column in agent_sessions table (JSON blob)

## Existing Code References
- storage.rs: agent_sessions table (DNA storage)
- patterns.rs: Detection events (tool usage, error counts)
- ingest.rs: capture pipeline (activity metrics source)

## Configuration
```toml
[session_dna]
enabled = true
embedding_dim = 8              # PCA embedding dimensionality
similarity_threshold = 0.85    # For clustering
k_neighbors = 10               # For predictions
min_sessions_for_pca = 50      # Before PCA is fitted
pca_refit_interval = 100       # Re-fit PCA every N new sessions
```

## Dependencies
- Enhanced by BOCPD (entropy, unique_ratio features)
- Enhanced by pattern detection (tool usage, error counts)
- Standalone computation otherwise

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `dna_incremental_update`: Single incremental DNA update (one capture cycle's worth of data: update activity fractions, output stats, pattern counts) must complete in <5us.
- `dna_embedding_projection`: Projecting a raw feature vector through the PCA matrix (17 features -> 8 dimensions) must complete in <1us.
- `dna_cosine_similarity`: Computing cosine similarity between two 8-dimensional embeddings must complete in <100ns.
- `dna_knn_search_100`: K-nearest-neighbor search (K=10) over 100 session embeddings must complete in <50us.
- `dna_knn_search_1000`: K-nearest-neighbor search (K=10) over 1000 session embeddings must complete in <500us.
- `dna_pca_fit_100`: Fitting PCA on 100 session feature vectors (17 dimensions) must complete in <10ms.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "session_dna" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_fingerprint_stability`: For any session with incrementally added data (generate random capture cycles: 10-100 cycles with random output rates, entropy values, pattern counts), the DNA fingerprint must change monotonically less per update as more data accumulates (the L2 norm of the delta between consecutive DNA snapshots must decrease on average). This ensures the fingerprint stabilizes over a session's lifetime.
- `proptest_embedding_normalization`: After PCA projection, the embedding vector must have finite values (no NaN/Inf) for any input feature vector with values in -1e6..1e6. Generate random 17-dimensional feature vectors.
- `proptest_similarity_bounds`: Cosine similarity must always be in [-1.0, 1.0] for any two non-zero embedding vectors. Generate random 8-dimensional vectors.
- `proptest_knn_consistency`: For K=1, the nearest neighbor of a session to itself must be itself (similarity = 1.0). For any set of sessions, the KNN result must contain exactly K sessions (or all sessions if fewer than K exist).
- `proptest_pca_reconstruction`: For any session feature vector, projecting to the 8-dimensional embedding and reconstructing back to 17 dimensions must have reconstruction error less than the sum of the discarded eigenvalues. Generate random feature vectors after PCA is fitted.
- `proptest_cold_start_similarity`: In pre-PCA mode (raw normalized features), similarity between two identical sessions must be 0.0 (L2 distance) and similarity between maximally different sessions must be bounded. Generate pairs of random feature vectors.

## Cross-References
- **wa-283h4.9** (Spectral fingerprinting): Session DNA and spectral fingerprinting are complementary fingerprinting approaches. Session DNA captures high-level behavioral features (activity patterns, tool usage, error rates) while spectral fingerprinting captures fine-grained temporal patterns via FFT (output periodicity, burst frequency spectra). The two can be combined: spectral features can be appended to the DNA feature vector before PCA projection, creating a richer "extended DNA" that captures both behavioral and temporal signatures.
- **wa-1qz1.2** (BOCPD): BOCPD-detected regime shifts define the activity burst boundaries used in Session DNA computation. When BOCPD detects a change-point, the DNA updater uses that as a burst boundary (incrementing burst_count, updating mean_burst_duration_s and mean_idle_duration_s). The change-point posterior probability also contributes to a "volatility" feature in the DNA, capturing how frequently the session's behavior shifts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- DNA feature vector computed incrementally per session
- Pre-PCA fallback using normalized raw features for cold start
- 8-dimensional PCA embedding for similarity computation (once fitted)
- K-nearest-neighbor predictions for duration and success
- Session clustering via cosine similarity
- wa robot session-dna <session_id> CLI command
- Unit tests: DNA computation from synthetic capture data
- Unit tests: pre-PCA fallback similarity (verify reasonable results with <50 sessions)
- Integration test: cluster similar sessions, verify predictions
- Criterion benchmarks meeting latency targets

 {"id":"wa-1qz1.2","title":"BOCPD agent state transition detector","description":"## Goal
Implement Bayesian Online Change-Point Detection (BOCPD) to detect agent state transitions that regex pattern matching cannot catch, enabling detection of novel failure modes and subtle behavioral shifts.

## Background & Motivation
patterns.rs (4,468 lines) uses a 3-stage pipeline: Bloom filter → Aho-Corasick anchor matching → regex extraction. This is excellent for KNOWN patterns (rate limits, errors, prompts) but fundamentally cannot detect:
- An agent stuck in an infinite loop (producing output that looks normal)
- Subtle degradation in output quality (slower, more repetitive)
- Novel failure modes from new agent versions
- Gradual drift from productive to unproductive behavior

BOCPD detects these by monitoring the STATISTICAL PROPERTIES of output, not the content. When the distribution of output metrics changes, BOCPD flags a change-point with formal posterior probability.

## Technical Design

### BOCPD Algorithm (Adams & MacKay 2007)
Maintain run length posterior P(rₜ|x₁:ₜ) where rₜ is the run length since the last change-point:

1. For each new observation xₜ:
   - Growth: P(rₜ=r+1|x₁:ₜ) ∝ P(xₜ|rₜ=r+1) × P(rₜ₋₁=r) × (1-H)
   - Change: P(rₜ=0|x₁:ₜ) ∝ P(xₜ|rₜ=0) × Σᵣ P(rₜ₋₁=r) × H
   - Where H is the hazard function (prior on change-point rate, default 1/200)

2. Predictive likelihood P(xₜ|rₜ) uses conjugate prior:
   - For output rate: Normal-Gamma conjugate (mean + variance tracking)
   - For entropy: Beta conjugate
   - For character distribution: Dirichlet-Multinomial

### Per-Pane Feature Vector (computed every capture cycle)
- `output_rate`: lines per second (float)
- `byte_rate`: bytes per second (float)
- `entropy`: Shannon entropy of character distribution (float, 0-8 bits)
- `unique_line_ratio`: fraction of lines that are unique (float, 0-1)
- `ansi_density`: fraction of bytes that are ANSI escape sequences (float, 0-1)

### Change-Point Events
When P(rₜ=0) exceeds detection_threshold (default 0.7):
- Emit a `bocpd.change_point` event with:
  - Posterior probability of change
  - Old regime statistics (mean rate, entropy, etc.)
  - New regime statistics
  - Evidence for the shift (which features changed most)

### Integration with Pattern Engine
BOCPD runs IN PARALLEL with regex pattern detection, not as a replacement:
- patterns.rs handles known patterns (regex-matched events)
- BOCPD handles unknown patterns (statistical anomalies)
- Both feed into the event system
- Combined evidence: if BOCPD detects a change AND patterns.rs detects a known event, confidence increases

### Galaxy-Brain Transparency
```
┌─ BOCPD Change-Point Detected (Pane 7) ──────────────────────────┐
│ P(change-point) = 0.94                                           │
│                                                                   │
│ Run length posterior: P(r=0|data) = 0.94, P(r=1|data) = 0.03    │
│                                                                   │
│ Regime shift:                                                     │
│   output_rate: 12.3 lines/s → 0.4 lines/s  (strongest signal)   │
│   entropy: 4.2 bits → 2.1 bits              (significant)        │
│   unique_line_ratio: 0.82 → 0.15            (significant)        │
│                                                                   │
│ 💡 Agent appears to have entered a repetitive loop state.        │
│    Low entropy + low unique ratio = repeating same output.       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation Location
- New file: crates/wa-core/src/bocpd.rs
- Integration: ingest.rs computes features per capture cycle, feeds to BOCPD
- Events: bocpd.change_point, bocpd.regime_shift, bocpd.anomaly

## Existing Code References
- patterns.rs: Detection, DetectionContext (parallel detection system)
- ingest.rs: PaneCursor delta extraction (feature computation point)
- events.rs: Event bus (where BOCPD events are published)
- storage.rs: output_segments (raw data for feature computation)

## Configuration
```toml
[bocpd]
enabled = true
hazard_rate = 0.005           # Prior: expect change every ~200 observations
detection_threshold = 0.7     # Min posterior for change-point event
feature_window_secs = 5       # Window for computing per-pane features
min_observations = 20         # Minimum before detection starts
```

## Dependencies
- None (standalone detection system)
- Complements patterns.rs (related, not dependent)

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `bocpd_single_update`: Single observation update through the BOCPD model must complete in <50μs (target: <30μs typical). Benchmark with Normal-Gamma conjugate prior, run length truncated at 200.
- `bocpd_feature_vector`: Per-pane feature vector computation (entropy, unique ratio, ANSI density) from a 1KB output chunk must complete in <100μs.
- `bocpd_batch_100_panes`: Update 100 pane BOCPD models sequentially must complete in <5ms.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "bocpd" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_hazard_rate_robustness`: For hazard_rate in 0.001..0.1, the model must remain numerically stable (no NaN/Inf in posteriors) after 1000 random observations drawn from Normal(0,1).
- `proptest_conjugate_prior_consistency`: After N observations from a known distribution, the posterior predictive mean must converge toward the true mean (within 2σ/√N) for N in 10..500.
- `proptest_run_length_normalization`: After any sequence of observations, the run length posterior must sum to 1.0 (within f64 epsilon tolerance).

### LabRuntime DPOR Concurrency Tests
- `concurrent_stream_processing`: Using LabRuntime with DPOR exploration, verify that multiple panes feeding BOCPD updates concurrently (via shared event bus) produce the same change-point detections regardless of interleaving order. Model 3 panes with 20 observations each, assert deterministic detection set.
- `concurrent_bocpd_and_pattern_engine`: Verify that BOCPD updates and pattern engine detections running concurrently on the same pane data do not interfere (no lost events, no duplicate events).

## Cross-References
- **wa-283h4.9** (Spectral fingerprinting): BOCPD change-point events feed into spectral fingerprinting as regime boundaries — when BOCPD detects a transition, the spectral fingerprinter can segment the FFT analysis at that boundary for cleaner frequency decomposition.
- **wa-1qz1.10** (Behavioral DNA): BOCPD-detected regime shifts define the activity burst boundaries used in Session DNA computation (burst_count, mean_burst_duration_s). The change-point posterior probability also contributes to the DNA embedding as a volatility feature.

## Acceptance Criteria
- BOCPD model with Normal-Gamma conjugate prior for output rate
- Per-pane feature computation (rate, entropy, unique ratio, ANSI density)
- Change-point events emitted when posterior exceeds threshold
- Galaxy-brain card via wa robot API showing regime shift details
- Runs parallel to regex engine with no interference
- Unit tests: synthetic time series with known change-points
- Integration test: detect agent entering stuck-loop state
- Criterion benchmarks meeting latency targets (<50μs per update)
- Proptest suites for parameter robustness and numerical stability

 {"id":"wa-1qz1.3","title":"VOI-optimal capture scheduler","description":"## Goal
Replace FrankenTerm's heuristic polling schedule with a Value-of-Information (VOI) optimal capture scheduler that provably minimizes uncertainty about pane states per unit of polling cost.

## Background & Motivation
Current polling in tailer.rs uses a fixed 1.5x exponential backoff with min/max bounds. bd-9dp proposes fixed tiers (200ms/2s/5s/10s/30s). Both are heuristics — they work but aren't optimal.

VOI scheduling asks: "Which pane, if polled RIGHT NOW, would give me the most useful information?" This is provably optimal because it maximizes expected information gain per unit cost.

## Technical Design

### VOI Computation
For each pane i at time t:
  VOI(i,t) = [H(Sᵢ|data_old) - E[H(Sᵢ|data_new)]] × W(i) / C(i)

Where:
- H(Sᵢ|data_old) = entropy of current state belief (higher = more uncertain)
- E[H(Sᵢ|data_new)] = expected entropy after polling (lower = more informative)
- W(i) = importance weight (from Bayesian classifier or manual priority)
- C(i) = cost of polling this pane (mux round-trip time, ~1-5ms)

### State Belief Model
Maintain a categorical distribution over pane states:
  P(state=s|data) for s ∈ {Active, Thinking, Idle, RateLimited, Error, Stuck}

Updated via Bayesian rule when new data arrives:
  P(state=s|data_new) ∝ P(observation|state=s) × P(state=s|data_old)

Entropy naturally decays (increases) over time without observations:
  H(t + Δt) = H(t) + Δt × drift_rate (entropy grows linearly with staleness)

### Scheduling Algorithm
```
loop {
    let pane = panes.max_by_key(|p| p.voi());
    if pane.voi() < min_voi_threshold {
        sleep(min_poll_interval);
        continue;
    }
    capture_pane(pane);
    update_beliefs(pane, observation);
}
```

### Integration with Existing Systems
- Replaces the adaptive polling logic in tailer.rs
- Uses backpressure.rs to adjust C(i) under pressure (cost increases when queues are full)
- Uses pattern detections to update P(observation|state) likelihood
- Falls back to bd-9dp fixed tiers if VOI computation disabled

### Backpressure Integration
Under backpressure, the cost C(i) increases:
  C_effective(i) = C_base(i) × backpressure_multiplier(tier)

This naturally reduces polling frequency under load — high-information panes still get polled, but the bar is higher.

### Galaxy-Brain Transparency
```
┌─ VOI Scheduler Status ──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Active panes: 52  |  Polls/sec: 18.3  |  Mode: VOI-optimal     │
│                                                                  │
│ Top 5 by VOI:                                                   │
│  Pane 12: VOI=0.83 (H=2.1, W=1.0, C=2ms)  [last: 340ms ago]  │
│  Pane 7:  VOI=0.71 (H=1.8, W=0.9, C=3ms)  [last: 1.2s ago]   │
│  Pane 31: VOI=0.45 (H=1.5, W=0.7, C=2ms)  [last: 890ms ago]  │
│  Pane 3:  VOI=0.12 (H=0.3, W=1.0, C=2ms)  [last: 200ms ago]  │
│  Pane 44: VOI=0.08 (H=0.2, W=0.5, C=5ms)  [last: 4.2s ago]   │
│                                                                  │
│ Bottom 5 (low VOI → rarely polled):                             │
│  Pane 19: VOI=0.001 (H=0.01, W=0.1) [last: 28s ago] [Dormant] │
│                                                                  │
│ 💡 Polling budget spent where it reduces uncertainty most.       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation Location
- Modify: crates/wa-core/src/tailer.rs (replace adaptive polling with VOI)
- New: crates/wa-core/src/voi.rs (VOI computation, belief model)
- Integration: Use pattern detections as observation updates

## Existing Code References
- tailer.rs: PaneTailer (current adaptive polling), CaptureScheduler (budget enforcement)
- backpressure.rs: BackpressureTier (cost multiplier source)
- patterns.rs: Detection events (observation updates for belief model)

## Configuration
```toml
[scheduler]
mode = "voi"                  # "voi" | "tiered" (fallback to bd-9dp)
min_voi_threshold = 0.01      # Below this, don't poll
entropy_drift_rate = 0.1      # Entropy growth per second without data
min_poll_interval_ms = 50     # Floor on polling frequency
max_poll_interval_ms = 30000  # Ceiling (even low-VOI panes get polled)
```

## Dependencies
- Related to bd-9dp (tiered rates) — VOI is the principled replacement
- Related to wa-3bin (smart priority) — uses Bayesian classification output as W(i)
- Uses backpressure tiers for cost adjustment

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `voi_single_pane_computation`: VOI computation for a single pane (entropy calculation + belief update) must complete in <1μs. Benchmark with 6-state categorical distribution.
- `voi_scheduling_round_50_panes`: Full scheduling round (compute VOI for 50 panes, select max, update beliefs) must complete in <100μs.
- `voi_scheduling_round_500_panes`: Full scheduling round for 500 panes must complete in <1ms.
- `voi_entropy_drift_update`: Batch entropy drift update for 500 panes must complete in <50μs.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "voi" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_voi_ordering_invariant`: For any set of pane states (generated via proptest), the pane with highest entropy and lowest cost must always have the highest VOI (when importance weights are equal). Generate 2-20 panes with random H, W, C values.
- `proptest_entropy_monotonicity`: For any pane, entropy must monotonically increase over time without observations. Generate random initial entropy in 0.0..3.0, drift_rate in 0.01..1.0, verify H(t+dt) >= H(t) for dt > 0.
- `proptest_belief_update_normalization`: After any Bayesian belief update with random observation likelihoods, the posterior must sum to 1.0 (within f64 epsilon). Generate random likelihood vectors for 6 states.
- `proptest_backpressure_cost_monotonicity`: Higher backpressure tiers must always produce higher effective cost, which must reduce VOI. Generate random base costs and backpressure multipliers.

## Cross-References
- **wa-283h4.8** (Entropy-aware capture scheduling): This bead provides the theoretical VOI foundation that wa-283h4.8 extends with spectral analysis — wa-283h4.8 adds FFT-based frequency detection to identify panes with periodic output patterns, allowing the VOI scheduler to anticipate information arrival times rather than relying solely on entropy drift.

## Acceptance Criteria
- VOI computation for each pane (< 0.1ms overhead)
- Provably polls highest-information-gain pane first
- Entropy-staleness tracking with configurable drift rate
- Backpressure integration (cost adjustment)
- Fallback to fixed tiers when VOI disabled
- Galaxy-brain card via wa robot API
- Unit tests: synthetic scenarios with known optimal polling order
- Benchmark: compare VOI vs fixed tiers on recorded workload traces
- Criterion benchmarks meeting latency targets

-{"id":"wa-1qz1.4","title":"Conformal prediction for resource forecasting","description":"## Goal
Implement split conformal prediction for FrankenTerm resource metrics, providing distribution-free prediction intervals with formal coverage guarantees for RSS, CPU, and I/O.

## Background & Motivation
FrankenTerm currently has no resource forecasting. The mux server can consume 76GB RSS over 23 days with no warning. By adding conformal prediction, we get mathematically guaranteed prediction intervals:
"With 95% probability, RSS will be between 52GB and 68GB in 2 hours."

Unlike parametric forecasting (which assumes a distribution), conformal prediction is DISTRIBUTION-FREE — it provides valid coverage guarantees with NO assumptions about the data generating process.

## Technical Design

### Split Conformal Prediction
1. Collect calibration set: recent resource observations {(x₁,y₁), ..., (xₙ,yₙ)}
   - xᵢ = feature vector (current RSS, pane_count, output_rate, uptime, hour_of_day)
   - yᵢ = actual RSS value at horizon (e.g., 30 min later)

2. Train simple point predictor ŷ = f(x) on training split (70% of data)

3. Compute nonconformity scores on calibration split (30%):
   sᵢ = |yᵢ - f(xᵢ)| for each calibration point

4. For new prediction at coverage level 1-α:
   - Sort calibration scores: s₍₁₎ ≤ s₍₂₎ ≤ ... ≤ s₍ₙ₎
   - Find q = ⌈(1-α)(n+1)⌉-th score
   - Prediction interval: [f(x_new) - q, f(x_new) + q]

### Coverage Guarantee (Theorem)
For exchangeable data, conformal prediction provides:
  P(y_new ∈ C(x_new)) ≥ 1 - α

This holds regardless of the underlying distribution. No parametric assumptions needed.

### Point Predictor
Use exponential smoothing with trend (Holt's method):
- Level: ℓₜ = α×yₜ + (1-α)(ℓₜ₋₁ + bₜ₋₁)
- Trend: bₜ = β(ℓₜ - ℓₜ₋₁) + (1-β)bₜ₋₁
- Forecast: ŷₜ₊ₕ = ℓₜ + h×bₜ

Holt's method is lightweight (O(1) per update, O(1) memory) and captures trends well.

### Multiple Forecasting Horizons
Generate intervals for: 30min, 1h, 2h, 4h, 8h
Each horizon has its own calibration set and nonconformity scores.

### Metrics Forecasted
- RSS (mux server memory): Primary concern
- CPU utilization (per-pane and aggregate)
- Disk I/O (write rate for SQLite storage)
- Capture queue depth (backpressure indicator)

### Alert Integration
When prediction interval upper bound exceeds alarm threshold:
- RSS upper bound > 80% of available memory → trigger proactive snapshot
- CPU upper bound > 90% → suggest reducing pane count
- Queue depth upper bound > 80% capacity → pre-emptive backpressure

### Galaxy-Brain Transparency
```
┌─ Conformal Resource Forecast ────────────────────────────────────┐
```

Implement split conformal prediction for FrankenTerm resource metrics, providing distribution-free prediction intervals with formal coverage guarantees for RSS, CPU, and I/O.

## Background & Motivation
FrankenTerm currently has no resource forecasting. The mux server can consume 76GB RSS over 23 days with no warning. By adding conformal prediction, we get mathematically guaranteed prediction intervals:
"With 95% probability, RSS will be between 52GB and 68GB in 2 hours."

Unlike parametric forecasting (which assumes a distribution), conformal prediction is DISTRIBUTION-FREE — it provides valid coverage guarantees with NO assumptions about the data generating process.

## Technical Design

### Split Conformal Prediction
1. Collect calibration set: recent resource observations {(x₁,y₁), ..., (xₙ,yₙ)}
   - xᵢ = feature vector (current RSS, pane_count, output_rate, uptime, hour_of_day)
   - yᵢ = actual RSS value at horizon (e.g., 30 min later)

2. Train simple point predictor ŷ = f(x) on training split (70% of data)

3. Compute nonconformity scores on calibration split (30%):
   sᵢ = |yᵢ - f(xᵢ)| for each calibration point

4. For new prediction at coverage level 1-α:
   - Sort calibration scores: s₍₁₎ ≤ s₍₂₎ ≤ ... ≤ s₍ₙ₎
   - Find q = ⌈(1-α)(n+1)⌉-th score
   - Prediction interval: [f(x_new) - q, f(x_new) + q]

### Coverage Guarantee (Theorem)
For exchangeable data, conformal prediction provides:
  P(y_new ∈ C(x_new)) ≥ 1 - α

This holds regardless of the underlying distribution. No parametric assumptions needed.

### Point Predictor
Use exponential smoothing with trend (Holt's method):
- Level: ℓₜ = α×yₜ + (1-α)(ℓₜ₋₁ + bₜ₋₁)
- Trend: bₜ = β(ℓₜ - ℓₜ₋₁) + (1-β)bₜ₋₁
- Forecast: ŷₜ₊ₕ = ℓₜ + h×bₜ

Holt's method is lightweight (O(1) per update, O(1) memory) and captures trends well.

### Multiple Forecasting Horizons
Generate intervals for: 30min, 1h, 2h, 4h, 8h
Each horizon has its own calibration set and nonconformity scores.

### Metrics Forecasted
- RSS (mux server memory): Primary concern
- CPU utilization (per-pane and aggregate)
- Disk I/O (write rate for SQLite storage)
- Capture queue depth (backpressure indicator)

### Alert Integration
When prediction interval upper bound exceeds alarm threshold:
- RSS upper bound > 80% of available memory → trigger proactive snapshot
- CPU upper bound > 90% → suggest reducing pane count
- Queue depth upper bound > 80% capacity → pre-emptive backpressure

 {"id":"wa-1qz1.5","title":"Bayesian evidence ledger for pane classification","description":"## Goal
Replace wa-3bin's EWMA+threshold pane classification with full Bayesian posterior inference over pane states, providing evidence ledgers that show exactly WHY each classification decision was made.

## Background & Motivation
wa-3bin (Smart Pane Priority) currently uses EWMA with exponential decay for output rate tracking and threshold-based classification. This works but:
1. Thresholds are arbitrary (rate > 10.0 → High, else Medium)
2. No confidence measure (is it 51% or 99% sure it's "Active"?)
3. Not explainable (WHY was this pane classified this way?)
4. Doesn't combine evidence from multiple signals formally

Bayesian classification solves all four by maintaining full posterior distributions and evidence ledgers.

## Technical Design

### State Space
S = {Active, Thinking, Idle, RateLimited, Error, Stuck, Background}

### Evidence Sources (per pane)
Each evidence source contributes a log-likelihood ratio:
1. Output rate (from capture pipeline): lines/sec
2. Entropy of recent output (from BOCPD feature computation)
3. Pattern detections (from patterns.rs): error, rate_limit, thinking, etc.
4. Time since last output (from tailer.rs)
5. Scrollback growth rate (from storage.rs)

### Posterior Update
For each pane, maintain log-posterior:
  log P(S=s|evidence) = log P(S=s) + Σᵢ log P(evidenceᵢ|S=s)

Prior P(S=s) is the stationary distribution learned from historical data.

Likelihoods P(evidenceᵢ|S=s) are learned from labeled observations:
- P(output_rate=12.3|Active) from historical Active-labeled panes
- P(output_rate=12.3|Idle) from historical Idle-labeled panes

### Evidence Ledger
For each classification, record:
```json
{
  "pane_id": 7,
  "classification": "Stuck",
  "posterior": {"Active": 0.02, "Thinking": 0.05, "Idle": 0.08, "Stuck": 0.82, ...},
  "evidence_ledger": [
    {"source": "output_rate", "value": 0.3, "log_lr_stuck_vs_active": 3.2, "contribution": "strong_for_stuck"},
    {"source": "entropy", "value": 1.1, "log_lr_stuck_vs_active": 2.8, "contribution": "strong_for_stuck"},
    {"source": "unique_ratio", "value": 0.05, "log_lr_stuck_vs_active": 4.1, "contribution": "very_strong_for_stuck"},
    {"source": "pattern_detection", "value": "none", "log_lr_stuck_vs_active": 0.5, "contribution": "weak_for_stuck"}
  ],
  "bayes_factor_vs_next_best": 15.3,
  "timestamp": 1738000000
}
```

### Bayes Factor Interpretation
- BF < 3: Barely worth mentioning
- 3 < BF < 10: Substantial evidence
- 10 < BF < 30: Strong evidence
- 30 < BF < 100: Very strong evidence
- BF > 100: Decisive

### Learning from Feedback
When user manually overrides a classification (via wa robot priority set):
- Treat as labeled observation
- Update likelihood parameters via conjugate update
- System gets smarter over time

### Galaxy-Brain Transparency
```
┌─ Pane 7 Classification ─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ log P(Stuck|evidence) = -0.20 → P = 0.82                       │
│ log P(Active|evidence) = -3.91 → P = 0.02                      │
│                                                                  │
│ Evidence ledger:                                                 │
│   output_rate=0.3/s:  log LR(Stuck/Active) = +3.2  ███████░    │
│   entropy=1.1 bits:   log LR(Stuck/Active) = +2.8  ██████░░    │
│   unique_ratio=0.05:  log LR(Stuck/Active) = +4.1  █████████   │
│   no_pattern_match:   log LR(Stuck/Active) = +0.5  ██░░░░░░    │
│                                                                  │
│ Bayes factor vs Active: 15.3 (strong evidence)                  │
│                                                                  │
│ 💡 Repetitive low-entropy output with very low unique ratio.    │
│    Agent likely stuck in a loop.                                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation Location
- Modify: crates/wa-core/src/priority.rs (replace EWMA with Bayesian model)
- New: evidence ledger data structures in priority.rs
- Integration: wa robot priorities shows evidence ledgers

- patterns.rs: Detection events (evidence source)
- tailer.rs: PaneTailer last_output tracking (evidence source)

```toml
mode = "bayesian"              # "bayesian" | "ewma" (fallback)
prior_update_interval_secs = 300
```

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `bayesian_single_evidence_update`: Single evidence update (one source, one pane, 7 states) must complete in <10us (target: <5us typical). Benchmark with log-posterior update across all 7 states.
- `bayesian_full_classification`: Full classification cycle (5 evidence sources, posterior update, Bayes factor computation, evidence ledger construction) must complete in <50us per pane.
- `bayesian_batch_100_panes`: Classify 100 panes with full evidence ledgers must complete in <5ms.
- `bayesian_feedback_update`: Conjugate prior update from user feedback must complete in <20us.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "bayesian_classifier" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_posterior_normalization`: After any sequence of evidence updates (generate random log-likelihood ratios in -10.0..10.0 for 7 states, 1-20 evidence sources), the posterior must sum to 1.0 (within f64 epsilon after exp-normalize).
- `proptest_bayes_factor_consistency`: The Bayes factor between top two states must equal exp(log_posterior_1 - log_posterior_2). Generate random posteriors, verify BF matches.
- `proptest_evidence_ledger_completeness`: For any set of evidence sources, the evidence ledger must contain exactly one entry per source, and the sum of log-LR contributions must equal the total log-posterior shift. Generate 1-10 random evidence updates.
- `proptest_monotone_evidence`: Adding evidence that favors state S must never decrease P(S). Generate a random prior and a single evidence update with positive log-LR for state S, verify P(S) increases.
- `proptest_conjugate_update_convergence`: After N feedback observations (N in 5..100) all labeled as state S, the prior for state S must increase monotonically.

## Cross-References
- **wa-3bin** (Smart pane priority): This bead is the principled Bayesian replacement for wa-3bin's EWMA+threshold classification. The evidence ledger provides the explainability that wa-3bin's simple thresholds lack. wa-3bin's priority tiers (High/Medium/Low/Background) map to posterior-weighted importance scores that feed into the VOI scheduler (wa-1qz1.3).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Galaxy-brain card via wa robot API

- Unit tests: synthetic evidence sequences with known correct classifications

 {"id":"wa-1qz1.6","title":"Continuous backpressure severity function","description":"## Goal
Replace the 4-tier discrete backpressure FSM with a continuous severity function, enabling smooth throttling curves instead of step functions, with exponential smoothing for hysteresis.

## Background & Motivation
backpressure.rs (657 lines) uses a 4-tier FSM (Green/Yellow/Red/Black) with hardcoded thresholds (50%/75% capture, 60%/80% write). Transitions are discrete steps that cause abrupt behavior changes:
- Going from Green to Yellow instantly doubles idle polling intervals
- Going from Yellow to Red instantly pauses 50% of panes
- Fixed 2000ms hysteresis prevents flapping but can't adapt to workload patterns

A continuous severity function provides smooth transitions where throttling intensity is proportional to load.

## Technical Design

### Severity Function
  s(t) = sigmoid(k * (q(t) - theta))

Where:
- q(t) = exponentially smoothed queue ratio (capture or write, whichever is higher)
- theta = center threshold (default 0.60, where severity = 0.5)
- k = steepness parameter (default 8.0, controls how fast severity rises)
- sigmoid(x) = 1 / (1 + exp(-x))

### Exponential Smoothing (replaces fixed hysteresis)
  q_smooth(t) = alpha * q_raw(t) + (1-alpha) * q_smooth(t-1)

Where alpha = 2/(N+1), N = smoothing window in samples (default N=10, so alpha is approximately 0.18)

This provides natural hysteresis: rapid load increases are smoothed, preventing oscillation without a fixed delay.

### Throttling Actions (continuous)
Instead of discrete tier actions, each action has a continuous intensity:
- Polling backoff multiplier: 1.0 + 3.0 * s(t)  (range: 1x to 4x)
- Pane skip fraction: 0.5 * s(t)^2  (range: 0% to 50%, quadratic to avoid premature shedding)
- Pattern detection skip: 0.25 * s(t)  (range: 0% to 25%)
- Buffer limit: max_segments * (1 - 0.8 * s(t))  (range: 100% to 20% of normal)

### Backward Compatibility
The existing BackpressureTier enum is preserved for external consumers:
```rust
impl ContinuousBackpressure {
    pub fn equivalent_tier(&self) -> BackpressureTier {
        match self.severity() {
            s if s < 0.25 => Green,
            s if s < 0.60 => Yellow,
            s if s < 0.85 => Red,
            _ => Black,
        }
    }
}
```

### macOS Platform Fallback for Pressure Data
The severity function ingests system pressure metrics to weight the queue ratio. On Linux, this reads /proc/pressure/* (PSI - Pressure Stall Information) for CPU, memory, and I/O pressure. On macOS, PSI is not available. The implementation must provide a platform abstraction:

- **Linux**: Read /proc/pressure/cpu, /proc/pressure/memory, /proc/pressure/io to obtain avg10/avg60/avg300 stall percentages. Parse the "some" and "full" lines.
- **macOS**: Use the Mach host_statistics64() API with HOST_VM_INFO64_COUNT to obtain vm_statistics64 data. Derive memory pressure from (active + wired) / total physical memory. For CPU pressure, use host_processor_info() to compute aggregate CPU utilization. For I/O pressure, use IOKit's IOStatisticsGetReport or fall back to disk activity heuristics from host_statistics (pageins/pageouts rate).
- **Abstraction layer**: Define a `SystemPressure` trait with a single `fn read_pressure() -> PressureSnapshot` method. Implement `LinuxPsiPressure` (reads /proc/pressure/*) and `MacosMachPressure` (uses vm_statistics64/host_statistics64). Select implementation at compile time via `#[cfg(target_os)]`.
- **PressureSnapshot struct**: Contains cpu_pressure (0.0-1.0), memory_pressure (0.0-1.0), io_pressure (0.0-1.0), normalized to a common scale regardless of platform.

### Implementation Location
- Modify: crates/wa-core/src/backpressure.rs (add continuous model alongside discrete)
- New: crates/wa-core/src/pressure.rs (platform-abstracted pressure reading)
- Integration: tailer.rs reads severity directly instead of tier enum
- Backward compat: existing tier consumers get mapped tier

## Existing Code References
- backpressure.rs: BackpressureConfig, BackpressureTier, BackpressureState
- tailer.rs: uses BackpressureTier for polling adjustment
- degradation.rs: uses BackpressureTier for load shedding decisions

## Configuration
```toml
[backpressure]
mode = "continuous"           # "continuous" | "discrete" (current FSM)
center_threshold = 0.60       # Queue ratio where severity = 0.5
steepness = 8.0               # Sigmoid steepness
smoothing_window = 10         # EMA window in samples
```

## Dependencies
None -- standalone enhancement to backpressure.rs.

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `severity_computation`: Single severity function evaluation (sigmoid + EMA update) must complete in <50ns.
- `severity_with_pressure_read`: Severity computation including platform pressure read must complete in <10us (macOS vm_statistics64 call is typically <5us).
- `throttle_action_computation`: Computing all 4 throttle action intensities from severity must complete in <100ns.
- `tier_mapping`: Converting continuous severity to BackpressureTier must complete in <10ns.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "backpressure_continuous" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_severity_monotonicity`: For any two queue ratios q1 < q2 (both in 0.0..1.0), severity(q1) <= severity(q2). The sigmoid is monotonically increasing, so this must always hold regardless of steepness or center_threshold parameters.
- `proptest_severity_continuity`: For any queue ratio q and small delta (delta in 0.0001..0.01), |severity(q+delta) - severity(q)| < delta * k (Lipschitz bound where k is the steepness parameter). This ensures no discontinuities.
- `proptest_severity_range`: For any queue ratio q in 0.0..1.0 and any valid configuration (center_threshold in 0.1..0.9, steepness in 1.0..20.0), severity must be in [0.0, 1.0].
- `proptest_ema_convergence`: After N identical observations (N in 10..100) of value v, the smoothed value must converge to v within 0.01. Generate random v in 0.0..1.0.

 {"id":"wa-1qz1.7","title":"Adaptive watchdog thresholds via Kalman filter","description":"## Goal
Replace fixed heartbeat thresholds in watchdog.rs with Kalman-filtered adaptive thresholds that learn normal inter-heartbeat intervals and detect anomalies relative to observed behavior.

## Background & Motivation
watchdog.rs uses fixed thresholds: capture is stale after 5s, discovery after 15s, persistence after 30s, maintenance after 120s. These are reasonable defaults but:
1. Don't adapt to system load (under pressure, heartbeats naturally slow)
2. Can't distinguish "slightly slow" from "completely hung"
3. False alarms when the system is legitimately loaded

A Kalman filter estimates the true heartbeat interval and its variance, allowing the watchdog to set thresholds at mu + k*sigma (k standard deviations above normal).

## Technical Design

### Per-Component Kalman State
For each component (discovery, capture, persistence, maintenance):
- State: x = [interval, drift]^T (interval between heartbeats + trend)
- Measurement: z = actual observed interval
- Process noise Q: accounts for natural variation
- Measurement noise R: accounts for timing jitter

### Kalman Update (per heartbeat)
```
Predict:
  x_hat = F * x + B * u
  P_hat = F * P * F^T + Q

Update:
  K = P_hat * H^T * (H * P_hat * H^T + R)^-1
  x = x_hat + K * (z - H * x_hat)
  P = (I - K * H) * P_hat
```

For 1D (interval only, no drift): simplifies to scalar Kalman filter.

### Adaptive Threshold
  threshold(t) = x(t) + k * sqrt(P(t))

Where k controls sensitivity:
- k=2: ~95% of normal heartbeats within threshold (moderate sensitivity)
- k=3: ~99.7% within threshold (low false alarm rate)
- k=4: very conservative

### Health Status (refined)
Instead of binary Healthy/Degraded/Critical:
- z-score = (observed_interval - x(t)) / sqrt(P(t))
- z < 2: Healthy
- 2 <= z < 3: Degraded (unusual but plausible)
- 3 <= z < 5: Critical (very unlikely to be normal)
- z >= 5: Hung (essentially certain anomaly)

### Implementation Location
- Modify: crates/wa-core/src/watchdog.rs (add Kalman filter alongside fixed thresholds)
- Per-component KalmanState stored in HeartbeatRegistry

## Existing Code References
- watchdog.rs: HeartbeatRegistry, WatchdogConfig, HealthStatus
- 4 components: discovery (15s), capture (5s), persistence (30s), maintenance (120s)

## Configuration
```toml
[watchdog]
mode = "adaptive"             # "adaptive" | "fixed" (current)
sensitivity_k = 3.0           # Standard deviations for threshold
process_noise = 0.1           # Kalman Q (higher = more adaptive)
measurement_noise = 0.5       # Kalman R (higher = smoother estimates)
min_observations = 5          # Before switching from fixed to adaptive
```

## Dependencies
None -- standalone enhancement to watchdog.rs.

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `kalman_scalar_update`: Single scalar Kalman filter predict+update cycle must complete in <100ns.
- `kalman_threshold_computation`: Adaptive threshold computation (mu + k*sigma) must complete in <50ns.
- `kalman_zscore_health_status`: z-score computation and health status classification must complete in <50ns.
- `kalman_batch_4_components`: Update all 4 component Kalman filters (discovery, capture, persistence, maintenance) must complete in <500ns.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "kalman_watchdog" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_kalman_convergence`: For a stationary heartbeat interval (constant true interval in 1.0..120.0 with Gaussian noise sigma in 0.1..5.0), after N observations (N in 20..200), the Kalman estimate must be within 2*sigma/sqrt(N) of the true interval. Generate random true intervals and noise levels.
- `proptest_variance_positive_definite`: After any sequence of observations (values in 0.1..300.0, length 1..500), the Kalman variance P must remain strictly positive (P > 0). This is essential for numerical stability.
- `proptest_threshold_above_estimate`: The adaptive threshold must always be >= the Kalman estimate (since k >= 0). For any k in 0.0..10.0 and any filter state, verify threshold >= estimate.
- `proptest_zscore_ordering`: For observations sorted by distance from the estimate, z-scores must be monotonically non-decreasing. Generate random estimates, variances, and observation sequences.
- `proptest_health_status_ordering`: Healthy < Degraded < Critical < Hung. For any z-score sequence, the health status must be monotonically non-decreasing with increasing z-score.

## Cross-References
- **wa-2cha** (Mux server watchdog): This bead provides the adaptive threshold intelligence that wa-2cha's watchdog consumes. wa-2cha defines the watchdog architecture (heartbeat registry, health checks, recovery actions), while this bead replaces its fixed thresholds with learned Kalman-filtered thresholds. The z-score health status (Healthy/Degraded/Critical/Hung) maps directly to wa-2cha's recovery action tiers (log/alert/restart component/restart server).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Scalar Kalman filter per component tracking inter-heartbeat intervals
- Adaptive thresholds at mu + k*sigma
- z-score based health status
- Falls back to fixed thresholds during warmup period
- Unit tests: synthetic heartbeat sequences with known anomalies
- Integration test: slow heartbeat detection under simulated load
- Criterion benchmarks meeting latency targets
- Proptest suites for filter convergence and numerical stability","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:04.097844Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:43:19.867821Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.7","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:04.097844Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}

 {"id":"wa-3bja.2","title":"Ghostty memory architecture analysis — scrollback, cells, allocation","description":"## Goal
Analyze Ghostty's memory management architecture, focusing on how it handles scrollback buffers, terminal cell storage, and memory lifecycle — then compare with WezTerm's approach to identify improvement opportunities for FrankenTerm.

## Analysis Focus Areas

### Scrollback Storage
- How does Ghostty store scrollback lines? (WezTerm: VecDeque<Line>, 100% in-memory)
- Does it use disk paging, mmap, or ring buffers?
- What's the per-cell memory overhead? (WezTerm: ~24 bytes/cell)
- How does it handle scrollback limits and eviction?

### Terminal Cell Representation
- How are cells with attributes stored? (WezTerm: Cell struct with CellAttributes)
- Is there any cell deduplication or compression?
- How are wide characters (CJK) handled?
- How are hyperlinks stored?

### Allocation Patterns
- Does Ghostty use arena allocators or custom allocators?
- How does it handle allocation/deallocation of pane state?
- Are there any "never shrink" antipatterns like WezTerm's HashMap caches?

### Memory Pressure Handling
- Does Ghostty monitor memory usage?
- Does it have any eviction or GC mechanisms?
- How does it handle OOM situations?

## Deliverable
A comparison document identifying 3-5 concrete ideas FrankenTerm could adopt:
- Pattern name
- Ghostty approach (with file references)
- WezTerm's current approach
- FrankenTerm opportunity (what we'd change and expected impact)

## Dependencies
- Clone and inventory (sub-task 1)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Analysis covers all 4 focus areas
- At least 3 actionable improvement ideas identified
- Each idea has clear implementation path for FrankenTerm

## Cross-references
- **wa-2ahu0** (memory pressure engine): Ghostty's memory pressure handling findings should directly inform the design of FrankenTerm's memory pressure engine — especially eviction strategies, per-pane memory budgets, and OOM responses.
- **wa-3axa** (custom allocator): Ghostty's allocation patterns (arena allocators, cell storage layout) should inform whether FrankenTerm benefits from a custom allocator or can achieve the same wins through data structure changes alone.

## Notes
- Findings from this analysis should directly inform FrankenTerm's memory strategy. If Ghostty uses arena allocation for terminal cells, compare the expected benefit against wa-3axa's proposed custom allocator to avoid duplicating effort.

 {"id":"wa-3bja.3","title":"Ghostty I/O pipeline analysis — PTY reading, parsing, rendering","description":"## Goal
Analyze Ghostty's I/O pipeline — how it reads PTY output, parses terminal escape sequences, and renders — then compare with WezTerm's 2-thread-per-pane model to identify performance improvement opportunities for FrankenTerm.

## Analysis Focus Areas

### PTY Reading
- How does Ghostty read from PTY file descriptors? (WezTerm: dedicated read_from_pane_pty thread per pane)
- Does it use epoll/kqueue multiplexing?
- Does it use a thread pool or async I/O?
- What buffer sizes does it use?

### Parsing Pipeline
- How does Ghostty parse VT escape sequences? (WezTerm: dedicated parse_buffered_data thread per pane)
- Is parsing done in the same thread as reading?
- How does it handle parser state across chunks?
- Is the parser zero-copy?

### Rendering Pipeline
- How does Ghostty decide what to render after a parse?
- Is rendering batched or immediate?
- How does it handle rapid output (e.g., `cat large_file.txt`)?
- Does it coalesce rendering updates?

### Thread Model
- Total thread count for N panes? (WezTerm: 2N threads)
- How does it scale with many panes?
- Does it use work-stealing or fixed thread pools?

## Deliverable
Comparison document with 3-5 ideas for FrankenTerm's capture pipeline:
- How FrankenTerm's tailer/capture could adopt Ghostty's I/O patterns
- Potential thread count reduction
- Potential latency improvements

## Dependencies
- Clone and inventory (sub-task 1)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Analysis covers all 4 focus areas
- At least 3 actionable improvement ideas
- Each idea maps to specific FrankenTerm code (tailer.rs, mux_client.rs, etc.)

## Cross-references
- **wa-283h4.4** (io_uring pipeline): Ghostty's PTY reading strategy (epoll/kqueue multiplexing, buffer sizes, async patterns) should directly inform whether FrankenTerm's io_uring pipeline can achieve similar or better throughput. Compare Ghostty's approach with the io_uring design to identify synergies.
- **wa-2oph2** (SIMD processing): Ghostty's VT parser design (zero-copy, SIMD-friendly data layout) should inform FrankenTerm's SIMD escape sequence processing. If Ghostty's parser is already SIMD-optimized, document the specific techniques used.

## macOS Considerations
- Ghostty's I/O pipeline likely uses kqueue on macOS and epoll on Linux. Document both paths.
- io_uring is Linux-only; on macOS, FrankenTerm will need kqueue or GCD (Grand Central Dispatch) as the fallback for async I/O. Note any macOS-specific patterns Ghostty uses.

## Notes
- Findings from this analysis should inform FrankenTerm's I/O hot path design — the critical path from PTY read to screen update. Any latency reduction here directly improves perceived terminal responsiveness.

 {"id":"wa-3cyp","title":"[EPIC] WezTerm Performance Optimization for Agent Swarms","description":"## Goal
Optimize WezTerm + wa for intensive AI agent swarm workloads: 50+ concurrent panes on 256GB RAM / 32+ core servers, running 24/7 with Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini sessions.

## Background & Motivation
We observed severe WezTerm degradation under agent swarm workloads:
- **Memory**: 76GB RSS after 23 days (VecDeque<Line> in-memory scrollback, HashMap caches never shrink, ~5MB thread accumulation per pane spawn/terminate)
- **CPU**: 35-40% with 24+ panes (2 threads per pane, zstd compression on local socket, Lua callbacks in hot path: format-tab-title invoked 3714x per alert)
- **Stuck processes**: 60+ zombie `wezterm cli` processes from lock contention on Mux singleton, synchronous PDU protocol with no timeout
- **Protocol corruption**: UnitResponse when expecting ListPanesResponse, feedback loops generating 150K log lines in 20 seconds

These are wa-side improvements that work around WezTerm's limitations without forking WezTerm itself. They make wa resilient against the known failure modes.

## Sub-beads (Priority Order)
1. Connection pool for DirectMuxClient (bd-41w) — eliminate CLI subprocess spawning
2. CLI command timeout hardening (bd-6js) — kill_on_drop and orphan reaper
3. Tiered update rates (bd-9dp) — aggressive backoff for idle panes
4. Protocol error auto-recovery — reconnect on UnexpectedResponse
5. Mux server watchdog — health monitoring and auto-restart
6. Scrollback memory pressure mitigation — evict old content under pressure
7. Content-addressable output dedup — deduplicate repetitive agent output

## Architecture
These improvements layer on top of wa's existing infrastructure:
- Pool<C> (pool.rs) — generic connection pool with health checks
- DegradationManager (degradation.rs) — graceful degradation subsystem
